fr:mainmatter.fr:p: A bottom type is a type that has no values and therefore no constructors. It is often denoted by the symbol \bot .
A top type is a type that has all values as its members. It is often denoted by the symbol \top .

A bottom type can be used to represent the type of expressions that:
never return a value, such as infinite loops or exceptions.
are not well-typed, such as type errors.
are not inhabited, such as the empty set.

A bottom type can also be used to define a subtyping relation in a type system. For example, if we have a type system with a top type \top that has all values associated with it, we can define a subtyping relation such that \bot is a subtype of all types, and all types are subtypes of \top . This allows us to reason about the relationships between types in a more flexible way.

A top type can be useful in a type system for several reasons:
It can be used to represent the type of expressions that can have any value, such as a function that can return any type.
It can be used to represent the type of expressions that are not well-typed, such as type errors.
It can be used to represent the type of expressions that are inhabited by all values, such as the set of all possible values.

A top type can also be used to define a subtyping relation in a type system. For example, if we have a type system with a bottom type \bot that has no values associated with it, we can define a subtyping relation such that \bot is a subtype of all types, and all types are subtypes of \top . This allows us to reason about the relationships between types in a more flexible way.
